title: Pentagon's 7-Year Munitions Mobilization: The Industrial Cycle Defense Bulls Underprice
summary: Iran-war stockpile depletion plus seven-year framework agreements signed Q1 2026 lock in a 5-9x production ramp across 14 critical munitions, giving LMT/NOC/RTX multi-year backlog visibility the market is still discounting as a one-cycle headline.
catalyst: On April 22, 2026, the Pentagon's FY27 budget request asked for $52.9 billion for 14 critical munitions and a 188% bump in missile procurement — including raising PAC-3 MSE output from 357 to 3,203 rounds and Tomahawk from 207 to 1,005 per year [1][2]. This is paired with the May 28, 2026 CSIS finding that the U.S. burned through ~50% of its Patriot interceptor stockpile during the Iran war, with replenishment now stretched into 2029 [3].
thesis: Unlike past defense bull markets that priced one or two strong years, the U.S. industrial base is locked into 7-year framework agreements signed in Q1 2026 (Lockheed's PAC-3 MSE deal Jan 6, Raytheon's five-system MoU Feb 4) that compound through the decade [4][5]. The bottleneck is not demand but capacity, and capacity is held by three primes: LMT (interceptors and PAC-3/THAAD final assembly), NOC (solid rocket motors — the single physical chokepoint), and RTX (Tomahawk, AMRAAM, SM-3/SM-6). Each owns a sole-source position on weapons the Pentagon has now publicly designated 'critical.'
body_md:
## Setup

The U.S. left the seven-week Iran war in April 2026 with one of the largest interceptor depletions in its post-Cold-War history. A May 28 CSIS analysis concluded that the Pentagon expended **roughly half of its Patriot PAC-3 stockpile** in the campaign — more than 1,000 rounds — against an FY26 delivery rate of only **172 interceptors** [3][6]. THAAD inventories drew down to similar levels. The CSIS authors estimate full replenishment will not occur until **mid-to-late 2029** even under accelerated production [3].

This is not a soft 'modernization' cycle. On April 22, 2026, the Trump administration released a $1.5 trillion FY27 defense budget request — a 42% increase over FY26 spending — that carved out **$52.9 billion for a designated list of 14 critical munitions** [1][2]. The Pentagon's Munitions Acceleration Council (MAC) published the list publicly:

- **Interceptors**: PAC-3 MSE, THAAD, SM-3 Block IIA, SM-3 Block IB, SM-6
- **Cruise & strike**: Tomahawk (LACM and Maritime Strike variants), JATM, LCCM, JASSM-ER, LRASM, AMRAAM
- **Emerging**: Low-cost hypersonic strike weapons, PrSM Inc. 1

Budget justification documents call for **average production capacity increases of ~9x** across the list [2]. Patriot funding alone jumps from $1.6B in FY26 to **$12B in FY27** [1].

## Why Now

Three things changed in Q1 2026 that the market has not fully integrated:

**1. Seven-year framework agreements were signed.** On January 6, 2026, Lockheed Martin and the Department of War announced a landmark seven-year agreement to triple PAC-3 MSE output from 600 to 2,000 missiles per year [4]. On February 4, 2026, RTX's Raytheon segment signed **five parallel framework MoUs** covering Tomahawk (both variants), AMRAAM, SM-3 IB, SM-3 IIA, and SM-6 — each a seven-year commitment to expand capacity 2-4x [5]. These are not LOIs; they are production base agreements that the Pentagon must now fund and that the primes are pouring capex against today.

**2. The contracts have started flowing.** On April 10, 2026, Lockheed announced its first **$4.76 billion firm-fixed-price** contract under the new PAC-3 framework, with deliveries through June 30, 2030 [7]. Roughly 94% of the contract is foreign military sales — meaning these missiles are not being bought to sit in a depot but to backfill allied stockpiles drained in parallel.

**3. The Iran ceasefire is provisional.** The 60-day memorandum of understanding extending the April ceasefire was reportedly still awaiting Trump's signature as of May 28 [8]. Brent is sitting at $93–95 and PCE inflation just printed 3.8% — a three-year high — largely on Iran-driven oil shock [9]. Even if Iran goes quiet, Ukraine, Taiwan-contingency planning, and Israeli replenishment alone consume the projected output for years.

## Company-Specific Edge

### Lockheed Martin (LMT)
LMT is the cleanest interceptor pure-play. PAC-3 MSE production is being **tripled** (600 → 2,000) and THAAD is being **quadrupled** under separate landmark agreements [4][10]. LMT's record **$194 billion backlog** at YE25 represents 2.5x annual sales — and that's before the April $4.76B PAC-3 contract and FY27's nine-fold MSE round increase land [10]. Forward P/E of **19.2x** with a 76.9% ROE (juiced by buybacks but real cash generation underneath) makes it the cheapest prime relative to forward earnings revisions. The 2.23% dividend yield with a 62% payout ratio adds carry while the production ramp compounds.

### Northrop Grumman (NOC)
NOC owns the single most underappreciated chokepoint in the entire complex: **solid rocket motors**. After the Aerojet Rocketdyne acquisition by L3Harris, there are effectively only two qualified SRM suppliers in the U.S., and NOC is the larger of the two. NOC is doubling SRM output from **13,000 per year in 2024 to 25,000 by 2029**, with the new $100M Propulsion Innovation Center in Elkton, Maryland completing in H2 2026 [11]. Every Patriot, every Standard Missile, every Tomahawk needs propellant NOC produces. Q1 2026 backlog of **$95.6B** plus EPS of $6.14 (beat consensus of $6.05) at a **forward P/E of 18.5x** and 28.5% ROE makes this the highest-quality-per-multiple name in the trio.

### RTX (RTX)
RTX has the largest absolute exposure to the ramp via Raytheon. **Q1 2026 backlog hit $271B**, up from $268B at YE25 — meaning RTX is *still adding more book than it ships* despite Q1 revenue of $22.1B [12]. Tomahawk production is going from 90/year legacy → 1,000+/year under the Feb 4 framework, the steepest absolute ramp in the entire MAC list. RTX is investing $3.1B of CapEx in 2026 — a 20% YoY increase — concentrated in Tucson, Huntsville, and Andover [12]. The valuation is the catch: at 25.2x forward P/E, RTX is the most expensive of the three and carries lingering GTF engine recall noise in Pratt & Whitney. Position size accordingly.

## Numbers That Matter

| Metric | LMT | NOC | RTX |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward P/E | 19.2 | 18.5 | 25.2 |
| ROE | 76.9% | 28.5% | 11.0% |
| Net margin | 6.7% | 10.8% | 7.6% |
| Rev growth YoY | 9.1% | 4.4% | 12.1% |
| Backlog | $194B (2.5x sales) | $95.6B (2.2x sales) | $271B (3.4x sales) |
| Dividend yield | 2.23% | 1.65% | 1.43% |
| Key program ramp | PAC-3 3x, THAAD 4x | SRM 13K→25K | Tomahawk ~11x |
| AI Resilience (IOWN) | 8 | 8 | 8 |

All three score 8/10 on AI Resilience in the screener, reflecting the structural insulation of classified-environment defense work from AI displacement.

## Bear Case

The most credible pushback is *industrial base saturation*. The Pentagon can request a 9x production increase, but propellant capacity, ammonium perchlorate supply, ITAR-controlled machine tools, and cleared labor are real constraints — Hegseth himself disputed at a May budget hearing the framing that stockpiles are 'depleted' [13]. If primes can't execute the ramp, revenue stays linear while expectations get repriced down. A second risk: the FY27 budget is a request, not law. The $350B reconciliation gambit that funds part of the supplemental could fail [14]. Finally, framework MoUs are *not* book of business — they are capacity reservations the Pentagon may or may not pull on. Past administrations have walked back acceleration commitments before.

A softer risk: defense indices have already rerated. The Iran-strike day pop (LMT +3.4%, RTX +4.7%, NOC +6%) is in the price. The thesis here is not that the headline is unknown — it is that the *seven-year visibility* and the magnitude of the FY27 budget step-up are still being modeled as one-or-two-cycle items rather than a structural reset of the missile/munitions industrial base.

## Conclusion

The three named primes each own a non-substitutable position in the 14-weapon MAC list, sit on multi-year framework agreements signed in Q1 2026, are pouring CapEx against committed capacity expansion, and are funded by a budget request that — even at 70% Congressional pass-through — represents an ~8x step-up in critical interceptor procurement. LMT for valuation and interceptor purity, NOC for the SRM chokepoint, RTX for the largest absolute Tomahawk ramp despite valuation. Three-stock basket positioning with LMT/NOC weighted heavier than RTX captures the upside while limiting GTF-related single-name risk.

ticker_rationale.LMT: Triple PAC-3 MSE (600 → 2,000/yr) and quadruple THAAD under Jan 6 seven-year framework agreement; $194B record backlog at YE25 is 2.5x annual sales, and at 19.2x forward P/E with a 2.23% dividend yield this is the cheapest defense prime relative to forward production ramps already contracted.
ticker_rationale.NOC: Owns the solid-rocket-motor chokepoint — every interceptor and cruise missile on the MAC list runs on NOC propulsion. Doubling SRM output from 13,000 to 25,000 per year by 2029, $95.6B Q1 2026 backlog, 28.5% ROE at just 18.5x forward P/E — best quality-per-multiple in the basket.
ticker_rationale.RTX: Tomahawk ramping ~11x from 90 to 1,000+/yr under Feb 4 five-system Raytheon framework MoU; Q1 2026 backlog hit $271B (a 3.4x book-to-bill vs annualized revenue) and CapEx is up 20% YoY at $3.1B, but 25.2x forward P/E and lingering Pratt GTF noise warrants smaller position size.

risks: Industrial base saturation: ammonium perchlorate, qualified labor, and ITAR-controlled tooling could cap actual deliveries below contracted ramp rates, leaving revenue linear while multiples derate.
FY27 $1.5T budget request is not law — the $350B reconciliation package funding the munitions supplemental could fail in Senate negotiations or be cut materially.
Iran 60-day MoU holds and oil retraces toward $70s, removing one Hill-side political driver and softening the multi-year supplemental narrative even if base demand persists.
RTX-specific: residual Pratt & Whitney GTF engine recall liability creates non-defense cash drag that could offset commercial side of the segment ramp.
Framework MoUs are capacity reservations, not firm orders — Pentagon program offices may not exercise full multi-year quantities if administration priorities shift in 2028.
invalidation: Pentagon publicly cancels or descopes any of the five RTX Feb 4 framework MoUs or the Jan 6 LMT PAC-3 agreement.
FY27 critical munitions appropriation comes in below $35B (vs $52.9B requested) in the final enacted bill — that would signal real Congressional pushback.
NOC Propulsion Innovation Center opening slips beyond Q1 2027 (currently H2 2026), signaling SRM bottleneck cannot be relieved on the contracted schedule.
LMT trailing backlog falls below 2.0x annual sales for two consecutive quarters — meaning new orders are no longer outpacing recognition.
Sustained Iran-US comprehensive peace deal with verifiable nuclear inspections and full ceasefire — removes the urgency premium driving FY27 supplemental sizing.
Sector valuation: LMT trades above 26x forward P/E without an upward earnings revision — the multi-year ramp is then fully discounted.
